When Israel left Egypt, everything shifted. Not because the people had become worthy, but because God had chosen them. Psalm 114 holds up this moment as a pivot point in history—the moment when a nation of slaves became a sanctuary, a dominion set apart for the Lord. But notice what the psalmist emphasizes: it is not the people's courage or cunning that accomplished this. It is God's presence. The sea flees not from Israel's strength but from the God of Israel. The Jordan retreats not from military prowess but from the God of Jacob. Even the terrain itself responds—mountains skipping like rams, hills bounding like lambs—as creation itself bows before its Maker. This is not poetry meant to inspire through sentiment. It is testimony to the absolute sovereignty of God over all things, material and immaterial alike.

The psalmist pauses midway through to ask the creation itself a question: Why? Why did the sea flee? Why did the Jordan turn back? Why did the mountains skip? The answer lies not in the mechanisms of nature but in the presence of the One who made nature. God's presence is the reason. His appearance is the cause. And this presence is not abstract or theoretical—it is so undeniably real that it moves rock and water, that it makes the earth tremble. The God of Jacob, the God who appeared to Jacob in his wrestling, the God who has kept covenant with the patriarchs, is the God who is present now. His presence reorders reality itself.

What should your response be, then? The psalmist commands the earth to tremble before the Lord's presence. But you are not merely earth. You are one who has been called, like Israel, to belong to God. You have been purchased and set apart, not by your own strength but by His. The waters of your baptism, like the waters of the Red Sea and Jordan, testify to a deliverance you did not earn and could not accomplish alone. The rock that God split for Israel's thirst in the wilderness points forward to the Rock that was pierced for your salvation. The same God who parted seas and stilled rivers stands before you now. The question He presses into you is not whether you can be strong enough or faithful enough or worthy enough. The question is whether you will tremble at His presence, whether you will bow before the God who has already acted on your behalf.
